title: Insurance Agencies and Brokerages

role_judgments.rationale: Quoting, application intake and proposal preparation can be automated, but licensed advisory sales, complex placement and retention remain human-led.
Routine inquiries, certificates, endorsements and carrier follow-up are workflow-heavy; exception handling and client advocacy persist.
Policy and claim processing is largely structured document and data workflow, subject to carrier-system integration and review.
Analytics and reporting automate, while carrier negotiation, hiring and operating judgment do not.
Routine desk-file review is automatable; field assessment, negotiation and disputed claims remain human work.
General clerical document, inbox and data-entry work is highly susceptible to automation with supervised exceptions.
Risk triage and file assembly automate materially, while delegated appetite and carrier accountability retain human judgment.
Scheduling, quality tracking and workflow allocation automate; staff coaching and exception escalation remain.
Scheduling, formatting, correspondence and filing are highly automatable but executive support retains exceptions.
Campaign creation, lead scoring and routine analytics automate; positioning and relationship marketing remain human.
The unlisted wage tail likely blends accounting, compliance, IT and account-management work, with mixed automation exposure.

ai_replaceable_share.source_fact: No fetched source apportions automatable task fractions by the supplied SOC roles for NAICS 524210.
ai_replaceable_share.derivation: Judgment by role task mix in retail agency and brokerage operations; no source establishes these fractions.

pi_dist.rationale: Commission revenue and recurring renewals let agencies retain much of service-cost saving, but direct distribution and carrier leverage materially limit retention.
pi_dist.source_fact: First Connect reports that direct-to-consumer competition was the biggest factor affecting the agent distribution channel, rising from 37% to 43% year over year.
pi_dist.derivation: The cited survey establishes channel pressure, not savings capture. I therefore assign 0.65 as a judgment below full retention to reflect direct-channel substitution and carrier bargaining; the numeric value is not measured by the source.

citations.title: First Connect Study Reveals Carrier-Agent Friction Falling
citations.figure_quoted: Direct-to-consumer competition remained the single biggest factor impacting the agent distribution channel, rising from 37% to 43% year-over-year.
citations.scope: First Connect 2026 survey of 238 independent insurance agencies and 44 carrier/MGA partners; US-oriented agency-channel survey, not a NAICS-wide savings-capture measure.

pi_moat.rationale: Renewal relationships, fragmented carrier appetites and licensed advice protect complex commercial placement, but not simple personal or small-commercial business from direct and embedded channels.
pi_moat.source_fact: No fetched source quantifies the share of NAICS 524210 revenue protected from carrier-direct or embedded distribution.
pi_moat.derivation: Judgment for the dominant retail P&C and benefits-agency segment; no source establishes the selected protection probability.

t50_years.source_fact: No fetched source forecasts the date at which 50% of US NAICS 524210 firms will materially use AI in automatable functions.
t50_years.derivation: Starting from a 35% agent-use proxy and allowing for workflow integration, compliance review and carrier connectivity, I estimate four years to firm-level material adoption; no source establishes this forecast.

current_adoption_pct.source_fact: First Connect reports: "Only 35% of agents currently use AI in day-to-day operations."
current_adoption_pct.derivation: The source measures agents, whereas the required population is firms materially using AI. I use 35% unchanged as a conservative firm-adoption proxy, explicitly without claiming that agent and firm incidence are identical.

citations.title: First Connect Study Reveals Carrier-Agent Friction Falling
citations.figure_quoted: Only 35% of agents currently use AI in day-to-day operations
citations.scope: First Connect 2026 survey of 238 independent insurance agencies and 44 carrier/MGA partners; respondent measure is agents using AI, not firm-level NAICS 524210 adoption.

historical_analogs.value: Agency-management-system, comparative-rating and digital-self-service adoption are directional analogs: they improve agency throughput but do not by themselves establish AI labor displacement or timing.
historical_analogs.source_fact: No fetched longitudinal source supplies a directly comparable agency AI adoption curve.
historical_analogs.derivation: Qualitative analogy only; no source establishes a numerical historical translation.

owners_60plus_pct.source_fact: No fetched source reports the age-60-plus share of owners of US NAICS 524210 firms in the target EBITDA band.
owners_60plus_pct.derivation: Conservative industry judgment for owner-operated agencies; no source establishes the selected owner-age percentage.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: No fetched source directly documents a succession shortage for the defined NAICS and EBITDA target band; the formula therefore receives no shortage increment.

active_consolidators.source_fact: OPTIS reports that 95 firms announced acquisitions in 2025, compared with 104 in 2024.
active_consolidators.derivation: Selected value is the reported 2025 count of unique acquisition-announcing firms; it is a transaction-market measure, not a census of every potential consolidator.

citations.title: North American Agent & Broker 2025 Year-end Merger & Acquisition Report
citations.figure_quoted: The number of unique buyers continues its 4-year downward slide as 95 firms announced acquisitions in 2025 compared to 104 in 2024.
citations.scope: OPTIS Partners' North American agent-and-broker M&A report; unique firms announcing acquisitions in 2025, not restricted to the US NAICS target EBITDA band.

buy_mult.source_fact: No fetched source establishes an EV/EBITDA purchase multiple for $1-10M EBITDA NAICS 524210 targets after market-rate owner compensation.
buy_mult.derivation: Judgment for a normalized smaller independent-agency acquisition; excludes unnormalized SDE and contingent consideration. No source establishes 9.0x.

exit_mult.source_fact: MarshBerry states that privately held platform firms have held flat at an average of around 14x EBITDA for base purchase price at closing over the past three years.
exit_mult.derivation: I use the reported 14x private-platform base purchase price as the exit reference for a scaled platform, while excluding earn-outs. The source does not disclose target size, NAICS purity, EV construction, or market-rate owner-comp normalization; the selected 14.0x therefore remains a platform proxy rather than direct evidence for the target band.

citations.title: Clock Speed and The Landscape of Risk
citations.figure_quoted: Over the past three years private platform firms have held flat at an average of around 14x EBITDA for base purchase price at closing, and near 19x with an earn-out.
citations.scope: MarshBerry commentary on privately held insurance-broker platforms over the past three years; platform reference, not disclosed $1-10M EBITDA target transactions or owner-comp-normalized deal comps.

pricing_structure.value: The dominant retail-agency model earns carrier commissions and recurring renewal commissions rather than billing client service hours; commercial fees and carrier commission repricing are important exceptions.
pricing_structure.source_fact: No fetched primary source quantifies NAICS 524210's commission-versus-fee revenue mix or carrier repricing pass-through.
pricing_structure.derivation: Industry-model judgment used only to explain capture; no source establishes the selected description or revenue shares.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Commission-dominant retail distribution, with material variation by benefits, wholesale, MGA and large-commercial accounts

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: The role judgments place the greatest automation in processing and clerical work, so the Python-computed uplift should be material but constrained by producer and management judgment. The four-year adoption estimate means A remains materially below the immediate-adoption maximum; Python supplies uplift_pp.

terminal_value.justification: AI can reduce agency service cost and increase capacity, but the paid product is an intermediary commission pool. Complex commercial placement, renewals and licensed advice support durable demand, while direct-to-consumer, embedded and carrier automation can shrink the pool for simpler risks.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V uses only role-task judgments and A uses the adoption proxy plus timing judgment. OPTIS deal counts and MarshBerry multiples are used only for B/M inputs, not as evidence for V or A.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Commission-dominant revenue supports a pi_dist judgment above one-half because reducing service labor does not mechanically reduce billed hours. Direct-channel competition and carrier commission leverage reduce pi_dist to 0.65; weak structural protection for simpler risks limits pi_moat to 0.55.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A ranges from 5.0 at two years to about 1.43 at seven years.
C moves proportionally with pi_moat and determines how much cost saving is defensible.
The entry multiple directly changes M; target-band normalized transaction evidence was not fetched.

reviewer_note: Heterogeneous catch-all boundary: dominant judgment is retail independent P&C/benefits agencies; NAICS also includes life/annuity, personal-lines, MGA/MGU and wholesale models. The supplied 5-digit 52421 wage mix is a reasonable but not six-digit-pure proxy. DATASET_MISMATCH: the W-2 labor share may understate owner/producer economics where 1099 producers or owner distributions are material; injected value is unchanged, so V sensitivity follows the provided labor-share basis.
